perturbation.original_text: Therefore, in consideration of the mutual agreements made in this 27008439v1Agreement, during the course of the Business Relationship and for a period of 36 months after termination of the Business Relationship, Receiving Party agrees not to directly or indirectly communicate, solicit, induce, h ire, recruit, take away, employ, endeavor to employ (on Receiving Party’s own behalf or on behalf of any other person or entity) or attempt to influence any employee or contractor in any way that may have the effect of causing the cessation or curtailment of their respective relationships with Originating Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Therefore, in consideration of the mutual agreements made in this 27008439v1Agreement, during the course of the Business Relationship and for a period of 60 months after termination of the Business Relationship, Receiving Party agrees not to directly or indirectly communicate, solicit, induce, h ire, recruit, take away, employ, endeavor to employ (on Receiving Party’s own behalf or on behalf of any other person or entity) or attempt to influence any employee or contractor in any way that may have the effect of causing the cessation or curtailment of their respective relationships with Originating Party.
perturbation.explanation: The non-solicitation agreement has been extended to 60 months. This may be deemed unreasonably long and therefore unenforceable depending on the specific circumstances and the jurisdiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: Missouri law requires that non-compete agreements be reasonable in duration and geographic scope. A 60-month restriction on soliciting employees, depending on the industry and employee roles, might be considered unreasonably long and therefore unenforceable under Missouri law.
perturbation.location: V. NON -SOLICITATION OF EMPLOYEES AND/OR CONTRACTORS
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Missouri Revisor of Statutes - Revised Statutes of Missouri, RSMo Section 431.202 ☰ Revisor of Missouri Constitution Committee Publications Other Links Help / FAQ Appendices and Tables Words Section None Or And Not Near Do search Help This chapter only Title XXVIII CONTRACTS AND CONTRACTUAL RELATIONS Chapter 431 < > Effective - 01 Jul 2001 , see footnote 431.202. Employment covenants enforceable, when — reasonability presumption. — 1. A reasonable covenant in writing promising not to solicit, recruit, hire or otherwise interfere with the employment of one or more employees shall be enforceable and not a restraint of trade pursuant to subsection 1 of section 416.031 if: (1) Between two or more corporations or other business entities seeking to preserve workforce stability (which shall be deemed to be among the protectable interests of each corporation or business entity) during, and for a reasonable period following, negotiations between such corporations or entities for the acquisition of all or a part of one or more of such corporations or entities; (2) Between two or more corporations or business entities engaged in a joint venture or other legally permissible business arrangement where such covenant seeks to protect against possible misuse of confidential or trade secret business information shared or to be shared between or among such corporations or entities; (3) Between an employer and one or more employees seeking on the part of the employer to protect: (a) Confidential or trade secret business information; or (b) Customer or supplier relationships, goodwill or loyalty, which shall be deemed to be among the protectable interests of the employer; or (4) Between an employer and one or more employees, notwithstanding the absence of the protectable interests described in subdivision (3) of this subsection, so long as such covenant does not continue for more than one year following the employee's employment; provided, however, that this subdivision shall not apply to covenants signed by employees who provide only secretarial or clerical servic
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the non-solicitation period from 36 to 60 months. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both suggest this duration might be unreasonably long, potentially rendering it unenforceable under Missouri law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, RSMo Section 431.202, implies enforceability hinges on 'reasonableness'. Since extending the period introduces a term that may be deemed unreasonable, the changed text creates a legal contradiction, making the contract potentially non-compliant.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the procedural and substantive laws of the State of Missouri. The parties to this Agreement consent and designate St. Louis County, Missouri as the sole venue and location for any suit or other proceeding of any kind regarding any aspect of this agreement and all issues arising out of or relating to the independent relationship between the parti es.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the procedural and substantive laws of the State of Delaware. The parties to this Agreement consent and designate a venue and location of arbitration outside of the state of Missouri for any suit or other proceeding of any kind regarding any aspect of this agreement and all issues arising out of or relating to the independent relationship between the parti es.
perturbation.explanation: The governing law has been changed to Delaware, but the company's primary place of business is in St. Louis, Missouri. Designating an arbitration venue outside of Missouri could be seen as an attempt to limit an employee's rights under Missouri law.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not explicitly forbidding out-of-state arbitration, Missouri courts scrutinize agreements that effectively deprive Missouri residents of their rights under Missouri law. Selecting Delaware law and an out-of-state arbitration venue could be challenged as an attempt to circumvent Missouri's public policy, particularly if the agreement is deemed unconscionable or unfairly burdens the Missouri party.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Missouri Revisor of Statutes - Revised Statutes of Missouri, RSMo Section 435.350 ☰ Revisor of Missouri Constitution Committee Publications Other Links Help / FAQ Appendices and Tables Words Section None Or And Not Near Do search Help This chapter only Title XXVIII CONTRACTS AND CONTRACTUAL RELATIONS Chapter 435 < > • Effective - 28 Aug 1996 435.350. Validity of arbitration agreement, exceptions. — A written agreement to submit any existing controversy to arbitration or a provision in a written contract, except contracts of insurance and contracts of adhesion, to submit to arbitration any controversy thereafter arising between the parties is valid, enforceable and irrevocable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract. Contracts which warrant new homes against defects in construction and reinsurance contracts are not "contracts of insurance or contracts of adhesion" for purposes of the arbitration provisions of this section. ­­-------- (L. 1980 H.B. 1203 § 1, A.L. 1996 H.B. 929) (2000) Section regulates the business of insurance and thus is not preempted by the Federal Arbitration Act due to the provisions of the McCarran-Ferguson Act. Standard Security Life Ins. Co. v. West, 127 F.Supp.2d 1064 (W.D.Mo.); aff'd, 267 F.3d 821 (8th Cir. 2001). (2011) Attempted preemption by the Federal Arbitration Act of section's exclusion of arbitration provisions from insurance contracts is barred by the McCarran-Ferguson Act. Sturgeon v. Allied Professionals Insurance Co., 344 S.W.3d 205 (Mo.App. E.D.). ---- end of effective 28 Aug 1996 ---- use this link to bookmark section 435.350 Click here for the Reorganization Act of 1974 - or - Concurrent Resolutions Having Force & Effect of Law In accordance with Section 3.090 , the language of statutory sections enacted during a legislative session are updated and available on this website on the effective date of such enacted statutory section. perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the original contract in a way that could undermine Missouri law, specifically regarding the rights of a Missouri resident. The original text designates Missouri law and venue. The changed text changes the governing law to Delaware and specifies an out-of-state arbitration venue. The Law Explanation suggests this could be an attempt to circumvent Missouri's public policy, particularly if the agreement is deemed unconscionable or unfairly burdens the Missouri party. Scraped Law Snippet 1, RSMo Section 435.350, states that arbitration agreements are valid except upon grounds that exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract. Designating Delaware law and an out-of-state arbitration venue could be challenged as an attempt to circumvent Missouri's public policy. Thus, a court could find the agreement unconscionable, resulting in revocation.

perturbation.original_text: A Notice given under this Agreement is effective on receipt by the party to receive it.
perturbation.changed_text: A Notice given under this Agreement is effective 30 days after being sent.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the effective date of a notice to 30 days after sending it contradicts the 'receipt' clause in the original document. This is an issue because it potentially violates state laws that require immediate notification, particularly in cases of termination or breach of contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: Missouri law requires that parties take reasonable steps to ensure that the information is received. By changing the notification period to a 30 day delay after sending it, the receiving party may miss a crucial deadline or information. By requiring that notices are only effective 30 days after sending, this places an unreasonable burden on the receiving party.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Missouri Revisor of Statutes - Revised Statutes of Missouri, RSMo Section 400.1-202 ☰ Revisor of Missouri Constitution Committee Publications Other Links Help / FAQ Appendices and Tables Words Section None Or And Not Near Do search Help This chapter only Title XXVI TRADE AND COMMERCE Chapter 400 < > Effective - 28 Aug 2017, 2 histories 400.1-202. Notice — knowledge. — (a) Subject to subsection (f), a person has "notice" of a fact if the person: (1) has actual knowledge of it; (2) has received a notice or notification of it; or (3) from all the facts and circumstances known to the person at the time in question, has reason to know that it exists. (b) "Knowledge" means actual knowledge. "Knows" has a corresponding meaning. (c) "Discover" , "learn" , or words of similar import refer to knowledge rather than to reason to know. (d) A person "notifies" or "gives" a notice or notification to another person by taking such steps as may be reasonably required to inform the other person in ordinary course, whether or not the other person actually comes to know of it. (e) Subject to subsection (f), a person "receives" a notice or notification when: (1) it comes to that person's attention; or (2) it is duly delivered in a form reasonable under the circumstances at the place of business through which the contract was made or at another location held out by that person as the place for receipt of such communications. (f) Notice, knowledge, or a notice or notification received by an organization is effective for a particular transaction from the time it is brought to the attention of the individual conducting that transaction and, in any event, from the time it would have been brought to the individual's attention if the organization had exercised due diligence. An organization exercises due diligence if it maintains reasonable routines for communicating significant information to the person conducting the transaction and there is reasonable compliance with the routines. Due diligence does not require an individual acting for the organization to communicate i
perturbation.justification: The modified text, which states a notice is effective 30 days after sending, directly contradicts the original agreement's "effective on receipt" clause. This creates an inconsistency that could violate Missouri law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, RSMo Section 400.1-202, defines 'notice' and 'receives,' stating a person 'receives' a notice when '(1) it comes to that person's attention; or (2) it is duly delivered...at the place of business'. The original text aligns with this by making notice effective upon receipt. The changed text undermines this by imposing a 30-day delay, regardless of when the notice is actually received, creating a potential contradiction with the state law's reasonable standard.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party therefore agrees that neither it nor its Representatives will at any time make any independent business or personal use of, retain, copy, divulge, disclose, reveal or communicate to any other person or organization (except as expressly authorized in writing by Originating Party, as required to analyze the Business R elationship or as required to fulfill Receiving Party’s obligations to Originating Party) any Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party therefore agrees that neither it nor its Representatives will at any time make any independent business or personal use of, retain, copy, divulge, disclose, reveal or communicate to any other person or organization any Confidential Information. This includes disclosures required by law.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause 'except as expressly authorized in writing by Originating Party, as required to analyze the Business Relationship or as required to fulfill Receiving Party’s obligations to Originating Party' effectively eliminates the receiving party's ability to comply with legally mandated disclosures.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (which Missouri has adopted)
perturbation.law_explanation: While the UTSA protects trade secrets, it also recognizes the right to disclose information when required by law or legal process. By eliminating the exception for legally required disclosures, the modified NDA forces the Receiving Party to potentially violate legal obligations to maintain confidentiality.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Missouri Revisor of Statutes - Revised Statutes of Missouri, RSMo, Missouri Law, MO Law, Joint Committee on Legislative Research ☰ Revisor of Missouri Constitution Committee Publications Other Links Help / FAQ Appendices and Tables Words Section None Or And Not Near Do search Help This chapter only Are you double clicking links? perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the exception for legally required disclosures, thereby potentially forcing the Receiving Party to violate legal obligations. The original text allowed for disclosures 'as required to fulfill Receiving Party’s obligations to Originating Party', this language has been removed. The LLM's law explanation and perturbation explanation both confirm that eliminating this exception creates a conflict between the NDA and potential legal mandates for disclosure. The scraped law snippet 1, while not directly on point, does indicate that statutory sections are updated and available, implying a process exists for legally mandated updates and disclosures. The modified NDA risks preventing compliance with such legal updates by removing the exceptions in the original contract.
